nodes.name: International Organizations
nodes.description: Major international organizations — founding, purpose, headquarters

items.prompt: Which humanitarian organization, founded in Geneva in 1863 after its founder witnessed the carnage at the Battle of Solferino, uses an emblem that is the inverse of the Swiss flag?
alternates: International Committee of the Red Cross
items.explanation:
Founded 17 February 1863 in Geneva by Henry Dunant and four others; the world’s oldest humanitarian organization
Dunant witnessed 40,000 casualties at the Battle of Solferino (1859) and wrote *A Memory of Solferino* (1862) proposing neutral relief societies
Five-person founding committee: Dunant, Gustave Moynier, General Henri Dufour, Dr. Louis Appia, Dr. Theodore Maunoir
Emblem is a red cross on white background — the inverse of the Swiss flag — chosen at the 1863 Geneva conference
First Geneva Convention signed 22 August 1864 by 12 states, establishing rules for treatment of wounded soldiers
Dunant won the first Nobel Peace Prize in 1901 (shared with Frederic Passy), despite being nearly destitute at the time
Three components today: ICRC (conflict zones), International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (disasters), and national societies in 191 countries
items.cardFront: 1863, Geneva, Henry Dunant, inverse Swiss flag emblem

items.prompt: Which organization, founded in Paris in 1894 by Pierre de Coubertin and headquartered in Lausanne since 1915, governs the Summer, Winter, and Youth editions of a major global sporting event?
items.explanation:
Founded 23 June 1894 at the first Olympic Congress in Paris by Pierre de Coubertin; Demetrios Vikelas of Greece was the first president
Moved headquarters from Paris to Lausanne, Switzerland in 1915, where it remains today on the shores of Lake Geneva
Coubertin revived the ancient Greek Olympic Games; the first modern Olympics were held in Athens in 1896 with 241 athletes from 14 nations
Kirsty Coventry (Zimbabwe) elected 10th president in March 2025 — the first woman and first African to lead the organization
Coventry won seven Olympic swimming medals, including back-to-back golds in the 200m backstroke (Athens 2004, Beijing 2008) — the most decorated Olympic swimmer from Africa
The Olympic Charter grants the committee sole authority over the Games, including selection of host cities and sports on the program
Not-for-profit organization; generates revenue primarily through broadcasting rights and sponsorship deals
items.cardFront: 1894, Lausanne, Pierre de Coubertin, governs the Olympics

items.prompt: Which international body, established on 10 January 1920 and headquartered in Geneva, was the first intergovernmental organization dedicated to maintaining world peace but dissolved in 1946 after failing to prevent a second global war?
items.explanation:
Founded 10 January 1920 at the Paris Peace Conference; headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland
Brainchild of U.S. President Woodrow Wilson, included in his Fourteen Points (1918) — yet the U.S. Senate rejected membership, fatally weakening it
Wilson won the 1919 Nobel Peace Prize for his role as the League’s leading architect
Peaked at 58 member states (September 1934–February 1935); lacked its own military force and relied on collective diplomacy
Major failures: could not stop Japan’s invasion of Manchuria (1931), Italy’s conquest of Ethiopia (1935), or the outbreak of World War II
Dissolved 18 April 1946; transferred assets, archives, and the Palais des Nations in Geneva to the newly formed United Nations
First worldwide intergovernmental organization — established precedents for international courts, health agencies, and labor standards that the UN inherited

items.prompt: Which international law enforcement body, originally established in Vienna in 1923 and now headquartered in Lyon, France, facilitates police cooperation across 196 member countries and is known for its color-coded alert notices?
items.explanation:
Founded 7 September 1923 in Vienna as the International Criminal Police Commission (ICPC) at a five-day International Police Congress
Dr. Johannes Schober, President of the Vienna Police, served as its first president (1923–1932)
Seized by the Nazis in 1938 after the Anschluss; revived in 1946 by Belgium, France, Scandinavia, the US, and the UK
Renamed International Criminal Police Organization (ICPO) in 1946; adopted the telegraphic name ‘Interpol’ in 1956
Headquarters moved from Vienna to Paris (1946), then Saint-Cloud (1966), then Lyon, France (1989), where it remains today
Issues color-coded notices: Red (wanted persons), Blue (identity information), Yellow (missing persons), Black (unidentified bodies)
196 member countries — the world’s largest international police organization; does not make arrests itself but coordinates between national police forces
items.cardFront: 1923, Lyon, international police cooperation, color-coded notices

items.prompt: Which international financial institution, conceived at the 1944 Bretton Woods Conference alongside the IMF and headquartered in Washington, D.C., was originally created to finance post-war European reconstruction and now focuses on development loans to reduce global poverty?
alternates: International Bank for Reconstruction and Development
items.explanation:
Conceived at the Bretton Woods Conference (July 1944) in New Hampshire; Articles of Agreement ratified 27 December 1945 by 21 countries
Formally the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD); began operations June 1946
Original mission: finance reconstruction of war-devastated Europe; first loan was $250 million to France in 1947
Headquartered in Washington, D.C. — Treasury Secretary Fred Vinson overruled plans for New York, fearing the ‘taint of international finance’
Now has 189 member countries; provides low-interest loans, grants, and technical assistance to developing nations
By convention, the World Bank president is always an American, while the IMF managing director is always a European
World Bank Group comprises five institutions: IBRD, IDA (poorest countries), IFC (private sector), MIGA (investment guarantees), ICSID (disputes)
items.cardFront: 1944, Washington D.C., Bretton Woods, development loans

items.prompt: Which international organization, founded on 24 October 1945 and headquartered in New York City, replaced a failed predecessor, has 193 member states, and features a Security Council with five veto-wielding permanent members?
items.explanation:
Charter signed 26 June 1945 in San Francisco by 51 original member states; entered into force 24 October 1945 (now observed as UN Day)
Replaced the League of Nations (1920–1946), which had failed to prevent World War II
Five permanent Security Council members (P5) with veto power: USA, UK, France, Russia (originally USSR), China
Security Council has 15 total members — 5 permanent and 10 non-permanent elected for two-year terms
Universal Declaration of Human Rights adopted 10 December 1948, primarily drafted by Eleanor Roosevelt
Currently 193 member states — effectively every recognized sovereign state; most recent member was South Sudan (2011)
Key specialized agencies: WHO (health), UNESCO (education/culture), UNICEF (children), UNHCR (refugees); headquarters on international territory in Manhattan
items.cardFront: 24 October 1945, New York, 193 members, P5 veto

items.prompt: Which Bretton Woods institution, formally established in December 1945 and headquartered in Washington, D.C., serves as the global lender of last resort and monitors exchange rates and balance-of-payments stability for its 191 member countries?
items.explanation:
Conceived at the Bretton Woods Conference (July 1944) based on ideas of John Maynard Keynes and Harry Dexter White
Formally established 27 December 1945 when 29 countries ratified its Articles of Agreement; headquartered in Washington, D.C.
191 member countries; mission is to foster global monetary cooperation, financial stability, and sustainable economic growth
Acts as a lender of last resort — provides emergency loans to countries facing balance-of-payments crises, often with strict conditions (structural adjustment)
By convention, the IMF managing director is always a European, while the World Bank president is always an American
Special Drawing Rights (SDRs): the IMF’s own reserve currency unit, created in 1969, based on a basket of five currencies (USD, EUR, GBP, JPY, CNY)
Controversial for ‘conditionality’ — loans often require austerity measures, privatization, and market liberalization that critics say harm developing nations
items.cardFront: 1945, Washington D.C., Bretton Woods, lender of last resort

items.prompt: Which specialized agency of the UN, established on 7 April 1948 and headquartered in Geneva, coordinates international public health efforts and led the successful campaign to eradicate smallpox in 1980?
items.explanation:
Constitution signed 22 July 1946 by 61 countries; entered into force 7 April 1948 (now observed as World Health Day)
Headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland; six regional offices worldwide; 194 member states
Led the global smallpox eradication campaign — declared successful 8 May 1980, the only human disease ever fully eradicated
G. Brock Chisholm (Canada) was the first Director-General; Andrija Stampar was the first World Health Assembly president
First priorities in 1948: malaria, tuberculosis, sexually transmitted infections, maternal and child health
Coordinated the global response to major epidemics: SARS (2003), Ebola (2014–2016), COVID-19 (declared pandemic 11 March 2020)
Preamble to its constitution contains the famous definition: ‘Health is a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being and not merely the absence of disease’
items.cardFront: 7 April 1948, Geneva, UN public health agency, eradicated smallpox

items.prompt: Which military alliance, formed on 4 April 1949 by 12 founding nations including the US, UK, Canada, and France, is headquartered in Brussels and invokes Article 5 for collective defense — an attack on one is an attack on all?
items.explanation:
Founded 4 April 1949 with the signing of the North Atlantic Treaty (Washington Treaty) by 12 nations
12 founding members: USA, UK, Canada, France, Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg, Norway, Denmark, Iceland, Italy, Portugal
Article 5 (collective defense): an armed attack against one member is considered an attack against all — invoked only once, after the 9/11 attacks (2001)
Headquartered in Brussels, Belgium; military headquarters (SHAPE) near Mons, Belgium
Created to counter the Soviet threat during the Cold War; the Soviets responded by forming the Warsaw Pact in 1955
Currently 32 member states — Finland joined in 2023, Sweden in 2024; expanded significantly after the Cold War into Eastern Europe
France withdrew from the integrated military command in 1966 under de Gaulle but remained a member; fully rejoined in 2009

items.prompt: Which intergovernmental organization, founded in Baghdad in 1960 by five oil-producing nations and now headquartered in Vienna, coordinates petroleum production policies and triggered the 1973 oil crisis with an embargo?
alternates: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
items.explanation:
Founded 14 September 1960 in Baghdad by five countries: Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and Venezuela
Headquarters initially in Geneva; moved to Vienna, Austria on 1 September 1965 after Switzerland declined to extend diplomatic privileges
1973 oil embargo: Arab members imposed an embargo against the US and others supporting Israel in the Yom Kippur War — oil prices quadrupled
1973 crisis transformed global energy politics; led to fuel rationing, speed limits, and a lasting shift toward energy independence in Western nations
Currently 12 member countries; members collectively produce about 38% of the world’s crude oil
OPEC+ (formed 2016) includes non-member allies like Russia to coordinate production; expanded the cartel’s market influence
Saudi Arabia is the de facto leader and largest producer within the group; Venezuela (a founder) has the world’s largest proven oil reserves
items.cardFront: 1960, Vienna, five founding oil nations, 1973 embargo

items.prompt: Which human rights organization, launched in 1961 after its British founder read about two Portuguese students jailed for toasting to liberty, campaigns for ‘prisoners of conscience’ worldwide and won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1977?
items.explanation:
Founded in London, July 1961, by British lawyer Peter Benenson after reading about two Portuguese students sentenced to seven years under Salazar’s dictatorship
Benenson’s article ‘The Forgotten Prisoners’ published in The Observer on 28 May 1961 launched the ‘Appeal for Amnesty’ campaign
Coined the term ‘prisoners of conscience’ — people imprisoned solely for their beliefs, ethnicity, or identity who have not used or advocated violence
Response was overwhelming; letter-writing groups formed in over a dozen countries within a year; formally named Amnesty International on 30 September 1962
Won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1977 for ‘contributing to securing the ground for freedom, for justice, and thereby also for peace in the world’
Headquartered in London; now has over 10 million supporters in more than 150 countries
Logo is a candle wrapped in barbed wire — symbolizing hope amid repression; designed in 1961 and based on the proverb ‘better to light a candle than curse the darkness’
items.cardFront: 1961, London, Peter Benenson, prisoners of conscience

items.prompt: Which regional bloc, established on 8 August 1967 in Bangkok by five Southeast Asian nations and headquartered in Jakarta, now has 11 member states including its newest member Timor-Leste (2025)?
alternates: The Association of Southeast Asian Nations
items.explanation:
Founded 8 August 1967 in Bangkok with the signing of the ASEAN Declaration (Bangkok Declaration)
Five founding members: Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand
Headquartered in Jakarta, Indonesia; formal Secretariat established at the 1976 Bali summit
11 member states: the five founders plus Brunei (1984), Vietnam (1995), Laos (1997), Myanmar (1997), Cambodia (1999), Timor-Leste (October 2025)
Combined population of ~680 million; would be the third-largest economy in Asia and fifth-largest in the world if counted as a single entity
Operates by consensus and non-interference in domestic affairs — the ‘ASEAN Way’ — which critics say limits its ability to address human rights abuses
ASEAN Regional Forum (ARF) is the key Asia-Pacific security dialogue; includes the US, China, Russia, Japan, and other outside partners
items.cardFront: 8 August 1967, Jakarta, five founding Southeast Asian nations

items.prompt: Which 27-member political and economic union, formally created by the Maastricht Treaty in 1993, evolved from a six-nation coal-and-steel community founded in the 1950s and introduced a common currency used by most of its members?
items.explanation:
Maastricht Treaty signed 7 February 1992, entered into force 1 November 1993 — formally created the EU from the earlier European Communities
Roots in the European Coal and Steel Community (1951) and the European Economic Community (EEC), founded by the Treaty of Rome (25 March 1957)
Original six EEC members: France, West Germany, Italy, Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg — the ‘Inner Six’
Euro currency launched 1 January 1999 (electronic) and 1 January 2002 (banknotes and coins); used by 20 of 27 member states in the eurozone
27 member states after Brexit — the UK left on 31 January 2020, the first and only country to withdraw from the EU
Headquartered in Brussels; key institutions include the European Commission (Berlaymont), European Council (Europa building), and European Parliament (Brussels and Strasbourg)
Schengen Area allows passport-free travel across 29 European countries (includes some non-EU states like Norway and Switzerland)
items.cardFront: Maastricht 1993, 27 members, euro currency, evolved from EEC

items.prompt: Which Geneva-based organization, established on 1 January 1995 through the Marrakesh Agreement, succeeded a 1947 trade framework and now governs rules for international commerce in goods, services, and intellectual property?
items.explanation:
Established 1 January 1995 pursuant to the Marrakesh Agreement, signed 15 April 1994 by 123 nations
Succeeded the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT), which had governed world trade from 1 January 1948
Headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, in the Centre William Rappard building on the shore of Lake Geneva
Key expansion over GATT: covers not just goods but also services (GATS) and intellectual property (TRIPS)
Dispute settlement mechanism is the ‘crown jewel’ — binding rulings that member states must comply with, unlike the toothless GATT system
Doha Development Round (launched 2001) stalled for over two decades — agriculture subsidies and developing-nation interests remain contentious
China’s accession on 11 December 2001 was a watershed moment; transformed global trade patterns and supply chains
items.cardFront: 1 January 1995, Geneva, succeeded GATT, trade rules

items.prompt: Which 55-member continental organization, launched in Durban on 9 July 2002 as the successor to a 1963 pan-African body focused on decolonization, is headquartered in Addis Ababa and aims to accelerate African political and economic integration?
items.explanation:
Launched 9 July 2002 in Durban, South Africa; successor to the Organisation of African Unity (OAU), founded 25 May 1963 in Addis Ababa
OAU was created by 32 signatory states focused on ending colonialism and apartheid; the AU shifted focus to economic integration and governance
Transition announced in the Sirte Declaration (9 September 1999) in Libya, championed by Muammar Gaddafi who envisioned a ‘United States of Africa’
Headquartered in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia; the African Union Commission building was controversially funded and built by China (opened 2012)
55 member states — every internationally recognized African country; 25 May is observed as Africa Day (anniversary of the OAU’s founding)
African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA), launched 2021, aims to create the world’s largest free trade area by number of countries
Unlike its predecessor, the AU can intervene in member states during war crimes, genocide, and crimes against humanity — a significant departure from the OAU’s non-interference doctrine
items.cardFront: 9 July 2002, Addis Ababa, successor to OAU, 55 members
